title: Alpha and Omega Semiconductor Unveils SmartClampTM Protected DrMOS Family for AI Servers and High-End GPUs
description: Alpha and Omega Semiconductor Limited (AOS) (Nasdaq: AOSL) a designer, developer, and global supplier of a broad range of discrete power devices, wide bandgap power devices, power management ICs, and modules, today announced the launch of the SmartClampTM family of protected DrMOS. Designed specifically for the extreme power demands of AI servers, data centers, and high-end graphics cards, the ... title: CYBERCOM seeks $593M in unfunded FY-27 priorities, citing gaps in munitions, AI and network defense
description: U.S. Cyber Command is asking Congress for nearly $600 million in additional funding in fiscal year 2027, warning that shortfalls in cyber "munitions," artificial intelligence and network defense capabilities are constraining its ability to sustain high-tempo operations, according to a document obtained by Inside Defense . In an unfunded priorities list submitted to lawmakers, CYBERCOM outlines $593 million in requirements it says were excluded from the Pentagon’s formal budget request due to topline constraints but are critical to mission execution. “Each item is a validated, mission-essential need that was not included in the President’s Budget due to fiscal constraints,” according to a letter to lawmakers from Gen. Joshua Reed, chief of U.S. Cyber Command and head of the National Security Agency. “These requirements directly support the National Defense Strategy by strengthening the Nation’s ability to compete, deter, and respond in cyberspace.” The largest portion of the request -- $229 million -- is aimed at scaling cyber operational capacity, including the development and deployment of cyber “munitions,” expanded training infrastructure and support for ongoing missions. The funding would address what the command describes as limitations in its ability to generate and sustain cyber effects at speed and scale. Another major component, $128 million, would support the integration of artificial intelligence into cyber operations, including investments in data infrastructure, compute capacity and analytic tools intended to accelerate decision-making and reduce operator workload. CYBERCOM is also seeking $101 million to bolster the Defense Department’s information network, funding it says would be used to improve visibility and defensive capabilities across the enterprise. The request pulls forward investments originally planned for fiscal year 2028, signaling what officials characterize as a more immediate risk to the network. The unfunded list further includes $46 million tied to the command’s evolving force structure under what officials have dubbed “CYBERCOM 2.0,” an effort to expand its role in force generation and capability development. That funding would support the establishment of a Cyber Warfare Innovation Center and other organizational changes aimed at accelerating capability delivery. A smaller request -- $11 million -- focuses on operator readiness, including cognitive, physical and psychological support programs, reflecting ongoing concerns about burnout within the cyber workforce. Additional items include $59 million to enhance reserve component cyber forces, particularly through improved equipment and mobilization support, and $ 19 million for facilities upgrades, including expanded space in the Pentagon and improvements to operational infrastructure in Texas. Unfunded Priorities Lists are statutorily required by Congress and are often used as blueprints for budgetary plus-ups. For the past two years , CYBERCOM has sought nothing in its UPL to Congress, noting its regular budgetary requests were sufficient. Notably, the regular DOD budget requested for this year is historically large at $1.5 trillion, with several combatant commands submitted empty UPLs. The Navy, which last year submitted a $7 billion UPL, this year sent Congress a list of around $600 million . The Army, meanwhile, has sent a UPL which includes $731 million for nine military construction projects.

title: Google Ghosts D.C. Desks To Feed Its AI Machine
description: Google is expected to consolidate D.C. offices as Alphabet raises 2026 capex to $180–$190B and prioritizes AI and cloud infrastructure.
